title: Central Bank Gold Reserves Survey 2026
gist: The World Gold Council's 2026 Central Bank Gold Reserves survey (76 respondents, a record) finds central banks accumulating ~1,000t/year over the past four years versus a 500t/decade prior average, with 89% expecting global central-bank gold reserves to rise over the next 12 months and a record 45% expecting their own reserves to grow. Respondents broadly anticipate lower US dollar reserve shares and rising gold holdings, and are increasingly diversifying gold vaulting away from Switzerland toward domestic and overseas storage.

insights.claim: Central banks have accumulated an average of 1,000 tonnes of gold per year over the past four years, double the 500 tonnes annual average of the preceding decade.
insights.speaker: World Gold Council
insights.so_what: A structural doubling of official-sector gold demand is a persistent bid that supports the gold price floor.
insights.quote: Central banks have accumulated an average of 1,000t of gold over the past four years, up significantly from the 500t average over the preceding decade.

insights.claim: The 2026 CBGR survey drew 76 responses, the highest participation on record in the nine years since the survey began and a year-over-year increase.
insights.speaker: World Gold Council
insights.so_what: Record survey participation signals deepening official-sector engagement with gold, making the findings more representative.
insights.quote: we set a new benchmark, drawing in 76 responses2 – surpassing the number of survey respondents in the previous year and the highest participation on record since our survey commenced nine years ago.

insights.claim: 89% of surveyed central banks expect global central bank gold reserves to increase over the next 12 months.
insights.speaker: World Gold Council
insights.so_what: Near-unanimous expectation of rising official gold reserves underpins a durable demand tailwind for bullion.
insights.quote: Respondents overwhelmingly (89%) believe that global central bank gold reserves will increase over the next 12 months.

insights.claim: A record 45% of respondents expect their own gold reserves to increase over the next 12 months, while 1% expect their institution's gold reserves to decrease and the majority of the rest expect no change.
insights.speaker: World Gold Council
insights.so_what: A record share of central banks planning to add gold themselves signals continued direct buying pressure.
insights.quote: a record 45% of respondents expect their own gold reserves will also increase over the same period. The majority of the remaining respondents indicated they expect no change while 1% expect their institution’s gold reserves to decrease.

insights.claim: 74% of surveyed central banks expect moderately or significantly lower US dollar holdings within global reserves over the next five years, while expecting gold holdings to increase and the euro and renminbi shares to stay unchanged.
insights.speaker: World Gold Council
insights.so_what: A central-bank consensus for a smaller dollar reserve share alongside more gold is a structural headwind for the dollar and tailwind for gold.
insights.quote: The majority of respondents (74%) see moderate or significantly lower US dollar holdings within global reserves over the next five years.

insights.claim: Half of respondents would fund new gold purchases through a domestic purchase programme in local currency, while 38% indicated funding via selling existing reserve assets.
insights.speaker: World Gold Council
insights.so_what: Domestic-currency purchase programmes mean much new central-bank gold demand bypasses open-market FX sales, while the 38% selling reserve assets can pressure those assets.
insights.quote: Half of respondents indicated through a domestic purchase programme in local currency, while 38% indicated through selling existing reserve assets.

insights.claim: The Bank of England remains the most popular gold vaulting location at 57%, followed by domestic storage at 49% and the Bank for International Settlements at 16% (a slight uptick), while the Swiss National Bank dropped to 6% from 12% in 2025.
insights.speaker: World Gold Council
insights.so_what: The sharp fall in Swiss National Bank vaulting preference and the rise in domestic and BIS storage flag a geopolitical-driven reshuffling of where official gold is held.
insights.quote: The Swiss National Bank saw a notable decline in preference, dropping to 6% from 12% in 2025.

insights.claim: Over the past 12 months 9% of central banks increased domestic gold storage and 10% diversified overseas storage locations, up sharply from 5% and 2% respectively a year earlier; for the coming 12 months 7% plan to increase domestic storage and 9% plan to diversify overseas storage.
insights.speaker: World Gold Council
insights.so_what: Accelerating repatriation and overseas diversification of vaulting reflects rising counterparty and geopolitical risk concerns among reserve managers.
insights.quote: 9% saying they have increased domestic storage and 10% saying they have diversified overseas storage locations in the past 12 months, compared with 5% and 2% respectively in last year’s survey.

insights.claim: Central banks cite gold's crisis-period performance, portfolio diversification, and inflation hedging as key reasons to hold gold, with gold as a geopolitical risk hedge and as part of a reserve diversification policy featuring as key reasons for increasing allocations.
insights.speaker: World Gold Council
insights.so_what: Gold demand is being driven by geopolitical-hedge and diversification motives rather than yield, suggesting it is rate-insensitive and sticky.
insights.quote: gold as a geopolitical risk hedge and gold as part of a reserve diversification policy also feature as key reasons for increasing allocations to gold.

insights.claim: The 2026 survey was conducted between 5 February and 19 May, with the majority of responses arriving after the start of the Middle East conflict, framing the findings around gold's role amid geopolitical turmoil.
insights.speaker: World Gold Council
insights.so_what: Survey timing captures real-time central-bank sentiment toward gold during an active geopolitical crisis, reinforcing the hedge thesis.
insights.quote: With the majority of responses coming in after the start of the Middle East conflict, this year’s survey contains insights on how central bankers view gold in the light of ongoing geopolitical turmoil.
